title: From Hormuz to the Maghreb: The Geopolitical Reach of a Gulf Crisis
gist: The February 2026 U.S.-Israeli war on Iran and closure of the Strait of Hormuz cut shipping through the strait by over 95 percent, spiking oil and fertilizer prices and exposing Morocco (a phosphate exporter dependent on Gulf sulfur and ammonia) as a loser and hydrocarbon-rich Algeria as a short-term winner and emergency European gas supplier—while entrenching Algeria’s rentier trap.

insights.claim: Passage of ships through the Strait of Hormuz fell by over 95 percent since March 2026 after Iran closed it and the U.S. blockaded Iranian ports, driving Brent crude up more than 50 percent against an estimated daily production shortfall of 14.5 million barrels.
insights.speaker: Yasmine Zarhloule
insights.so_what: Quantifies the supply shock—a 14.5 mb/d shortfall is a systemic oil-market event with global fertilizer and food knock-ons.
insights.quote: The passage of ships through the Strait of Hormuz had fallen by over 95 percent since March
insights.locator: The passage of ships through the Strait of Hormuz

insights.claim: The World Bank projects urea prices to rise nearly 60 percent in 2026 and the FAO warns fertilizer prices could average 15–20 percent higher in the first half, likely reducing yields for fertilizer-intensive crops like wheat, rice and maize in importers such as India and Brazil.
insights.speaker: Yasmine Zarhloule
insights.so_what: Fertilizer inflation transmits Gulf disruption into global food security—watch grain yields and food-price risk in major producers.

insights.claim: A Gulf/Middle East geopolitical disruption spreads globally even more than the Ukraine war did, because the Hormuz closure is a physical chokepoint that makes alternative sourcing far harder than the Russia-Ukraine shock.
insights.speaker: Yasmine Zarhloule
insights.so_what: Chokepoint physics make Hormuz shocks less substitutable than Ukraine—price geopolitical tail risk in the Gulf higher than in prior conflicts.
insights.locator: Indeed, the Hormuz crisis highlights the fact

themes.theme: Morocco: phosphate vulnerability and green-ammonia bet

insights.claim: Morocco holds roughly 70 percent of the world’s known phosphate reserves, but turning phosphate rock into fertilizer requires sulfur sourced almost entirely from the Gulf (which supplies ~44 percent of globally traded sulfur), leaving state-owned OCP’s ~3.7 million tons of annual sulfur imports exposed to Hormuz.
insights.speaker: Yasmine Zarhloule
insights.so_what: OCP’s feedstock dependency is a single-point-of-failure that Gulf disruption directly threatens—key for phosphate/fertilizer supply exposure.
insights.locator: The impact of the Hormuz crisis on Morocco

insights.claim: Morocco imports close to 90 percent of its energy needs, and Moroccan households had already absorbed diesel and gasoline prices rising to around $1.60 per liter in April 2026, squeezing consumer purchasing power and construction, real estate and petroleum-derived manufacturing.
insights.speaker: Yasmine Zarhloule
insights.so_what: High energy-import dependence makes Morocco a net loser from oil spikes, pressuring consumption and cyclical sectors.
insights.locator: Equally important to the discussion is Rabat’s broader energy dependence

insights.claim: OCP is diversifying sulfur routes through Kazakhstan and the Black Sea and shifting toward triple superphosphate fertilizer, which needs far less sulfur than DAP and MAP, while pursuing a $7 billion green-ammonia complex near Tarfaya that is not yet operational.
insights.speaker: Yasmine Zarhloule
insights.so_what: OCP’s hedge is real but incomplete—green ammonia won’t offset sulfur dependency and the flagship plant is still pre-production.
insights.locator: With Gulf supply curtailed, the OCP has had to diversify

insights.claim: OCP is pivoting sales beyond Europe, reportedly selling 90,000 tons of phosphate fertilizer to Latin American markets in March 2026 and negotiating additional deals with Indian companies.
insights.speaker: Yasmine Zarhloule
insights.so_what: Morocco is better positioned than rivals to keep exporting via Atlantic routes—relative resilience within the fertilizer trade.
insights.locator: Meanwhile, the OCP is looking beyond Europe

insights.claim: The EU debated an “emergency brake” to temporarily suspend its fertilizer Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (in force since January 1, 2026) under pressure from France, Italy, Croatia and Ireland, showing that rules meant to reward green producers like OCP can be altered under political pressure.
insights.speaker: Yasmine Zarhloule
insights.so_what: CBAM’s green premium is politically fragile—don’t underwrite green-fertilizer payoffs on regulatory certainty.
insights.locator: Morocco’s exposure to the crisis extends beyond physical supply chains

themes.theme: Algeria: hydrocarbon windfall and rentier trap

insights.claim: Algeria’s Sahara Blend crude jumped from below $70 a barrel in January 2026 to above $100 in March, its highest since the 2022 shock, before falling below $80 by mid-June on expectations the U.S.-Iran memorandum would reopen the strait.
insights.speaker: Yasmine Zarhloule
insights.so_what: Maps the price round-trip; Algeria’s fiscal windfall is real but transient and tied to the ceasefire holding.

insights.claim: Algeria is the EU’s third-largest gas supplier and second-largest pipeline supplier after Norway, with Italy and Spain each sourcing close to 30 percent of gas imports from Algeria and seeking to expand purchases after Iranian strikes on Qatar’s Ras Laffan LNG triggered a force majeure.
insights.speaker: Yasmine Zarhloule
insights.so_what: Algeria is Europe’s go-to emergency gas backstop—Sonatrach/Eni/Naturgy exposure gains geopolitical premium during Gulf crises.
insights.locator: This year, the Strait of Hormuz’s closure

insights.claim: Algeria’s ability to capitalize is capped by OPEC quotas (it raised output just 6,000 bpd in April 2026 to 977,000) and by declining fields: Hassi R’mel, nearly half of national gas output, is in long-term decline and required $2 billion in 2017 and $2.3 billion in 2024 to sustain, while domestic gas demand rises 4–5 percent a year.
insights.speaker: Yasmine Zarhloule
insights.so_what: Algeria cannot materially ramp exports—supply, not pipeline space, is the binding constraint, limiting how much it can substitute for the Gulf.
insights.locator: However, subject as it is to OPEC quotas

insights.claim: The hydrocarbon windfall entrenches Algeria’s rentier model and weakens its incentive to diversify, so even after Hormuz reopens Algeria is left with the same pre-crisis problems: dependence on oil and gas, limited fiscal margins, and an unfinished diversification agenda.
insights.speaker: Yasmine Zarhloule
insights.so_what: Algeria’s crisis “win” is a long-term trap—structurally negative for its non-oil economy despite short-term fiscal relief.
insights.locator: As for Algeria, its move to step in

insights.claim: Green-transition planning as a linear, technocratic sequence of targets, financing and certifications no longer holds when geopolitical crises accumulate, as governments hastily modify or suspend their own green strategies under emergency—reactivating subsidies, pivoting buyers, or ramping fossil-fuel output.
insights.speaker: Yasmine Zarhloule
insights.so_what: Peripheral-state energy transitions are crisis-contingent and reversible—discount linear decarbonization timelines for the Global South.
insights.locator: Together these dynamics complicate the story

insights.claim: The two Maghreb heavyweights respond in opposite directions to the same shock: Morocco absorbs supply shocks and accelerates its green pivot to reduce import dependence, while Algeria repositions as an emergency European fossil-fuel supplier and postpones its contested transition.
insights.speaker: Yasmine Zarhloule
insights.so_what: One shock produces divergent bets—Morocco as green-diversifier, Algeria as fossil-rentier—shaping which economy is more resilient long term.
insights.locator: Within this picture, Morocco and Algeria are tending in different directions
